summary: DHH criticizes a journalist at The Inquirer for writing an over-the-top preview of Doom 3 that compared its release to the Second Coming. He argues that this kind of hyperbolic tech journalism reveals shallow understanding rather than genuine enthusiasm. The piece points out that Id Software's own back catalog and several contemporary games were equally or more anticipated. DHH concludes that journalists who parachute into a topic for a single article can afford to burn all their hyperboles because they won't need to top themselves later.
key_points: Excessive hyperbole in journalism signals ignorance, not passion — 'nothing screams ignorance and wannabe more than overdoing and overplaying it'
Comparing Doom 3's release to the Second Coming ignores Id Software's own history of highly anticipated titles like Quake and Doom II
One-off journalists can blow all their rhetorical capital at once because they have no future credibility to protect
Genuine expertise requires proportionality — understanding where something fits in context rather than declaring everything unprecedented
money_quotes: The worst kind of journalists are those with a skin-deep interest and even less zeal for diligent research, but three times the reasonable amount of 'enthusiasm' and 'empathy'.
Nothing screams ignorance and wannabe more than overdoing and overplaying it.
They wade through one hyperbole after the next in a desperate attempt to show 'they get it'. But of course they don't.
Heralding Doom 3 with such fanfare discounts the entire history of Id Software and the gaming industry at large.
When you're only in the game for a single play, you can afford to blow all your hyperboles at once.
You don't have to worry about how to top a likening to the Second Coming when the next promising title arrives.
key_insight: Hyperbolic enthusiasm from journalists who lack deep subject knowledge betrays their ignorance rather than demonstrating passion, because real expertise demands proportionality and context.
